shorya1835's blog

Codeforces Round 1049 (Div. 2) Editorial
By shorya1835, history, 6 months ago,

2140A - Shift Sort

Idea: shorya1835 Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

2140A - Shift Sort

The minimum number of operations is equal to the number of misplaced 11's that are in the prefix of the string where 00's should be.

Let c0c0 be the total count of zeros in the string ss. In the sorted version, the first c0c0 characters will be 00's. The number of operations needed is simply the count of 11's in the prefix s[1…c0]s[1…c0].

The number of 11's in the prefix s[1…c0]s[1…c0] (misplaced 11's) is equal to the number of 00's in the suffix s[c0+1…n]s[c0+1…n] (misplaced 00's). Let this count be MM.

A single operation can fix one misplaced 11 and one misplaced 00.

  • Pick a misplaced 11 at index ii and a misplaced 00 at index kk.
  • Pick any index jj between them (i<j<ki<j<k).
  • The triplet of characters (si,sj,sk)(si,sj,sk) will be (1,bit,0)(1,bit,0).

A cyclic shift can always turn this into (0,bit,1)(0,bit,1), correcting both sisi and sksk.

  • If the triplet is (1,0,0)(1,0,0), a left shift results in (0,0,1)(0,0,1).
  • If the triplet is (1,1,0)(1,1,0), a right shift results in (0,1,1)(0,1,1).

If there is no bit between the misplaced 11 and 00 then there has to be a 11 to the right of misplaced 00 or 00 to the left of misplaced 11 (n≥3n≥3). We can choose this bit and perform a left shift or right shift respectively.

Since each operation fixes one such pair, the answer is MM.

Time Complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
using ll = long long;
 
int main(){
 
    int tt;
    cin >> tt;
 
    while(tt--){
 
        int n;
        string s;
        cin >> n >> s;
 
        int ans = 0;
 
        string p = s;
        sort(p.begin(), p.end());
 
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) if(s[i] != p[i]) ans++;
 
        cout << ans / 2 << '\n';
 
    }
    return 0;
}

2140B - Another Divisibility Problem

Idea: wakanda-forever Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

2140B - Another Divisibility Problem

Let's assume that the number yy has d(>0)d(>0) digits.

Now we try to express the number x#yx#y in an algebraic form.

x#y=x⋅10d+y=x⋅(10d−1)+(x+y)x#y=x⋅10d+y=x⋅(10d−1)+(x+y).

For x#yx#y to be divisible by x+yx+y, we have that x+yx+y must be a factor of x⋅(10d−1)x⋅(10d−1).

Now that we know d>0d>0, we can surely say that the number 3⋅x3⋅x is a factor of x⋅(10d−1)x⋅(10d−1). Thus, if we make x+y=3⋅xx+y=3⋅x, the condition is satisfied.

So, we can just choose yy to be equal to 2⋅x2⋅x.

Time Complexity: O(1)O(1) per testcase.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
int main(){
    int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        int x;
        cin >> x;
        cout << 2*x<< '\n';
    }
    return 0;
}

2140C - Ultimate Value

Idea: shorya1835 Preparation: wakanda-forever

Solution

2140C - Ultimate Value

Note that if Bob chooses two indices l,rl,r on his turn, then Alice can also choose the same indices l,rl,r on her next turn and this will increase the value of f(a)f(a) ultimately.

So, it remains optimal for Bob to end the game on his first turn.

Now there are two possibilities: Alice can end the game on her first turn, and Alice can choose two indices and Bob ends the game.

For the first case, finding f(a)f(a) is trivial.

Thus, we are left to find the maximum value of f(a)f(a) that Alice can attain on her first turn by choosing two indices. There are only two types of swaps between even index values and odd index values:

  • We swap even with even index or odd with odd index. In this case, only cost increases by absolute difference in index.
  • We swap even with odd or vice versa. In this case, the total change is |i−j|+2⋅(ai−aj)|i−j|+2⋅(ai−aj) where imod2=0imod2=0 and jmod2=1jmod2=1. This can be solved by maintain prefix minimum over odd and even indexes.

Time Complexity: O(n)O(n)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
using ll = long long;
 
int main(){
 
    int tt;
    cin >> tt;
 
    while(tt--){
 
        int n;
        cin >> n;
 
        vector<long long> a(n);
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            cin >> a[i];
        }
 
        ll ans = 0;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            if(i % 2) ans -= a[i];
            else ans += a[i];
        }
 
        ll init = ans;
 
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++) ans = max(ans, init + i - (i % 2));
 
        ll min_even = LLONG_MAX / 2, min_odd = LLONG_MAX / 2;
        for(int i = 0; i < n; i++){
            if(i % 2){
                ans = max(init + i + a[i] + a[i] - min_even, ans);
                min_odd = min(min_odd, i - a[i] - a[i]);
            }
            else{
                ans = max(init + i - a[i] - a[i] - min_odd, ans);
                min_even = min(min_even, i + a[i] + a[i]);
            }
        }
 
        cout << ans << '\n';
 
    }
 
    return 0;
}

2140D - A Cruel Segment's Thesis

Idea: shorya1835 Preparation: shorya1835

Solution

2140D - A Cruel Segment's Thesis

The question has a greedy solution. Let's first handle the case when nn is even, and then we will extend the solution to odd nn.

Since the lengths of the initial segments are fixed, we only need to maximize the contribution of the new segments formed. We will always set xixi to the left end and yiyi to the right end to maximize the answer. This means the answer is:

∑i=1n2ri−∑j=1n2lj,∑i=1n2ri−∑j=1n2lj,

where each segment contributes either its right end or its left end, but not both or neither.

A greedy approach is to initialize the answer as:

ans=∑i=1nri,ans=∑i=1nri,

and then sort the values of li+rili+ri. Subtract the lowest n2n2 values from the answer. This gives the best possible answer for even nn. The sequence of segments selected can also form a valid balanced parenthesis sequence, and thus a valid pairing.

This works because the answer given by this method is the maximum possible when choosing a subset of n2n2 segments to contribute their left ends. If the resulting sequence is not a valid balanced parenthesis sequence, then we can always improve it by swapping a segment from the left end to the right end and another from the right end to the left end.

For odd nn, we can run the above algorithm for ⌊n2⌋⌊n2⌋ and then check each segment to be the one segment that is left out, taking the maximum answer over all such possibilities or you can directly find out the left out segment in O(1)O(1).

Time Complexity: O(nlogn)O(nlog⁡n)

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
typedef long long ll;
typedef vector<int> vi;
typedef vector<ll> vl;
typedef set<int> si;
typedef pair<int, int> pi;
typedef pair<ll, ll> pl;

#define fo(i, a, b) for (ll i = a; i < b; i++)
#define F first
#define S second
#define pb push_back
#define mp make_pair
 
int main() {
    cin.tie(0)->sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.exceptions(cin.failbit);
    ll T, n;
    cin >> T;
    while (T--) {
        cin >> n;
        vector<pl> a(n);
        fo(i, 0, n)cin >> a[i].first >> a[i].second;
        ll ans = 0;
        fo(i, 0, n)ans += a[i].second - a[i].first + 1;
        ans += n / 2;
        vector<pair<ll, pl>> b(n);
        fo(i, 0, n) {
            b[i].second = a[i];
            b[i].first = a[i].first + a[i].second;
        }
        sort(b.begin(), b.end());
        if (n % 2 == 0) {
            fo(i, 0, n) {
                ans += a[i].second;
            }
            fo(i, 0, n / 2) {
                ans -= b[i].first;
            }
            cout << ans - n - n / 2 << "\n";
        }
        else {
            ll k = 0, w = 0;
            fo(i, 0, n) {
                w += a[i].second;
            }
            fo(i, 0, n / 2+1) {
                w-= b[i].first;
            }
            fo(i, 0, n / 2 + 1) {
                k = max(k, w + b[i].S.F);
            }
            w += b[n / 2].first;
            fo(i, n / 2 + 1, n) {
                k = max(k, w - b[i].S.S);
            }
            cout << ans + k  - n - n / 2 << "\n";
        }
    }
}

2140E1 - Prime Gaming (Easy Version)

Idea: Divine_Spark Preparation: shorya1835

Solution

2140E1 - Prime Gaming (Easy Version)

For the case of m=1m=1, there is only one possible valid configuration, the answer for which is 11.

For the case m=2m=2, To compute the answer for all possible starting states, we can reduce each state to a binary mask of length nn. For each mask, we independently compute the winning player using a regular DP with complexity O(n⋅2n)O(n⋅2n).

Alice will try to achieve 11 and Bob will aim for 00 in the following game where the meaning of:

  • bit 11: ai=2ai=2
  • bit 00: ai=1ai=1

If the final value for some mask is 11, it means ans=2ans=2. This can be calculated using dp[i][mask][p]dp[i][mask][p] which represents the value of a game with ii stones and configuration maskmask, where the current player to move is pp (p=1p=1 for Alice and p=0p=0 for Bob).

We can then iterate over off masks for games with nn stones and update answer accordingly.

For mask=0…2n−1mask=0…2n−1:
Ans←Ans+1+dp[n][mask][1]Ans←Ans+1+dp[n][mask][1]

Time Complexity: O(n⋅2n)O(n⋅2n)

Code
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
 
typedef long long ll;
int removebit(int n, int bit)
{
    int msk = (1 << 22);
    int filtermask = msk - (1 << bit);
    return ((n >> 1) & filtermask) | (n & ((1 << bit) - 1));
}
const ll MOD = 1e9+7;
class mint
{
public:
    ll val;
    static ll mod_exp(ll a, ll b)
    {
        ll res = 1;
        a = a % MOD;
        while (b > 0)
        {
            if (b % 2 == 1)
                res = (res * a) % MOD;
            b /= 2;
            a = (a * a) % MOD;
        }
        return res;
    }
    static ll gcdExtended(ll a, ll b, ll *x, ll *y)
    {
        if (a == 0)
        {
            *x = 0, *y = 1;
            return b;
        }
        ll x1, y1;
        ll gcd = gcdExtended(b % a, a, &x1, &y1);
        *x = y1 - (b / a) * x1;
        *y = x1;
        return gcd;
    }
    static ll modInverse(ll a)
    {
        ll x, y;
        ll g = gcdExtended(a, MOD, &x, &y);
        g++;
        ll res = (x % MOD);
        if (res < 0)
            res += MOD;
        return res;
    }
    mint() { val = 0; }
    mint(ll x)
    {
        val = x % MOD;
        if (val < 0)
            val += MOD;
    }
    mint &operator+=(const mint &other)
    {
        val += other.val;
        if (val >= MOD)
            val -= MOD;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint &operator-=(const mint &other)
    {
        val -= other.val;
        if (val < 0)
            val += MOD;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint &operator*=(const mint &other)
    {
        val = (val * other.val) % MOD;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint &operator/=(const mint &other)
    {
        val = (val * modInverse(other.val)) % MOD;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint &operator=(const mint &other)
    {
        val = other.val;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint operator+(const mint &other) const { return mint(*this) += other; }
    mint operator-(const mint &other) const { return mint(*this) -= other; }
    mint operator*(const mint &other) const { return mint(*this) *= other; }
    mint operator/(const mint &other) const { return mint(*this) /= other; }
    bool operator==(const mint &other) const { return val == other.val; }
 
    mint operator++()
    {
        ++val;
        if (val == MOD)
            val = 0;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint operator++(int)
    {
        val++;
        if (val == MOD)
            val = 0;
        return mint(val - 1);
    }
    mint operator--()
    {
        --val;
        if (val == -1)
            val = MOD - 1;
        return (*this);
    }
    mint operator--(int)
    {
        val--;
        if (val == -1)
            val = MOD - 1;
        return mint(val + 1);
    }
 
    // ^ has very low precedence, careful!!
 
    template <typename T>
    mint &operator^=(const T &other)
    {
        val = mod_exp(val, other);
        return (*this);
    }
    template <typename T>
    mint operator^(const T &other) const { return mint(*this) ^= other; }
 
    mint &operator^=(const mint &other)
    {
        val = mod_exp(val, other.val);
        return (*this);
    }
    mint operator^(const mint &other) const { return mint(*this) ^= other; }
 
    template <typename T>
    explicit operator T() { return (T)val; }
    template <typename T>
    friend mint operator+(T other, const mint &M) { return mint(other) + M; }
    template <typename T>
    friend mint operator-(T other, const mint &M) { return mint(other) - M; }
    template <typename T>
    friend mint operator*(T other, const mint &M) { return mint(other) * M; }
    template <typename T>
    friend mint operator/(T other, const mint &M) { return mint(other) / M; }
    template <typename T>
    friend mint operator^(T other, const mint &M) { return mint(other) ^ M; }
 
    friend std::ostream &operator<<(std::ostream &output, const mint &M) { return output << M.val; }
    friend std::istream &operator>>(std::istream &input, mint &M)
    {
        input >> M.val;
        M.val %= MOD;
        return input;
    }
};
 
const int maxn = 20;
const int maxm = 30;
 
ll modpow(ll a , ll b ) {
    ll res = 1 ;
    a %= MOD;
    while( b > 0 ) {
        if( b&1 ) res = ( res*a ) % MOD;
        a = ( a*a ) % MOD;
        b >>= 1 ;
    }
    return res ;
}
 
int main()
{   int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        bool pl = 1;
        int n, m;
        cin >> n >> m;
        int k,x;
        cin >> k;
        vector<int> good;
        for(int i=0;i<k;i++){
            cin >> x;
            good.push_back(x);
        }
        if(m==1){
            cout << 1<< "\n";
            continue;
        }
        vector<vector<vector<bool>>> dp(2, vector<vector<bool>>(n + 1));
        int cntt = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        {
    
            dp[0][i].resize(1 << i);
            dp[1][i].resize(1 << i);
            if (i == 1)
            {
                for (int j = 0; j <= 1; j++)
                {
                    for (int k = 0; k <= 1; k++)
                    {
                        dp[j][i][k] = k;
                    }
                }
            }
            else
            {
                for (int msk = 0; msk < (1 << i); msk++)
                {
                    bool ans0 = 1;
                    bool ans1 = 0;
                    for (auto x :good)
                    {
                        if (x <= i)
                        {
                            cntt++;
                            int finmsk = removebit(msk, x - 1);
                            ans0 &= dp[1][i - 1][finmsk];
                            ans1 |= dp[0][i - 1][finmsk];
                        }
                        else
                        {
                            break;
                        }
                    }
                    dp[0][i][msk] = ans0;
                    dp[1][i][msk] = ans1;
                }
            }
        }
        mint finans = 0;
        for (int r = 0; r < (1<<n); r++)
        {   
            finans += 1+dp[pl][n][r];
            
        }
        cout << finans << endl;
    }
}

2140E2 - Prime Gaming (Hard Version)

Idea: Divine_Spark Preparation:wakanda-forever, shorya1835

Solution

2140E2 - Prime Gaming (Hard Version)

To compute the answer for all possible starting states, we can reduce each state to a binary mask of length nn. For each mask, we independently compute the winning player using a regular DP with complexity O(n⋅2n)O(n⋅2n).

Alice will try to achieve 11 and Bob will aim for 00 in the following game where the meaning of:

  • bit 11: ai≥kai≥k
  • bit 00: ai<kai<k

If the final value for some mask is 11, it means ans≥kans≥k. This can be calculated using dp[i][mask][p]dp[i][mask][p] which represents the value of a game with ii stones and configuration maskmask, where the current player to move is pp (p=1p=1 for Alice and p=0p=0 for Bob).

For all r=1…nr=1…n, count the number of masks such that rr bits are on in the mask and the final answer is 11. Let this count be cnt[r]cnt[r]. The final answer can be calculated as follows:

For k=1…mk=1…m:
For r=1…nr=1…n:
Ans←Ans+(k−1)n−r⋅(m−k+1)r⋅cnt[r]Ans←Ans+(k−1)n−r⋅(m−k+1)r⋅cnt[r]

Time Complexity: O(n⋅2n+m⋅n)O(n⋅2n+m⋅n)

Code

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
const ll MOD = 1000000007;
int removebit(int n, int bit)
{
    int msk = (1 << 22);
    int filtermask = msk - (1 << bit);
    return ((n >> 1) & filtermask) | (n & ((1 << bit) - 1));
}

const int maxn = 20;
const int maxm = 30;
 
ll binpow(ll a,ll b,ll m = MOD){
    ll res = 1;
    while (b){
        if (b&1) res = (res*a)%m;
        a = (a*a)%m;
        b>>=1;
    }
    return res;
}
int main()
{   int t;
    cin >> t;
    while(t--){
        bool pl = 1;
        int n, m;
        cin >> n >> m;
        int k,x;
        cin >> k;
        vector<int> good;
        for(int i=0;i<k;i++){
            cin >> x;
            good.push_back(x);
        }
        vector<vector<vector<bool>>> dp(2, vector<vector<bool>>(n + 1));
        vector<vector<vector<int>>> cnt(2, (n + 1, vector<vector<int>>(n + 1, vector<int>(n + 1))));
        int cntt = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
        {
    
            dp[0][i].resize(1 << i);
            dp[1][i].resize(1 << i);
            if (i == 1)
            {
                for (int j = 0; j <= 1; j++)
                {
                    for (int k = 0; k <= 1; k++)
                    {
                        dp[j][i][k] = k;
                    }
                    cnt[j][1][1]++;
                }
            }
            else
            {
                for (int msk = 0; msk < (1 << i); msk++)
                {
                    bool ans0 = 1;
                    bool ans1 = 0;
                    for (auto x :good)
                    {
                        if (x <= i)
                        {
                            cntt++;
                            int finmsk = removebit(msk, x - 1);
                            ans0 &= dp[1][i - 1][finmsk];
                            ans1 |= dp[0][i - 1][finmsk];
                        }
                        else
                        {
                            break;
                        }
                    }
                    dp[0][i][msk] = ans0;
                    dp[1][i][msk] = ans1;
                    cnt[0][i][__popcount(msk)] += ans0;
                    cnt[1][i][__popcount(msk)] += ans1;
                }
            }
        }
        ll finans = 0;
        for (int r = 1; r <= n; r++)
        {   
            for(int k=1;k<=m;k++){
                finans += (((binpow(k-1,n-r)*binpow(m-k+1,r))%MOD) * (cnt[pl][n][r]))%MOD;
            }
        }
        cout << finans%MOD << endl;
    }
}
Bonus

Solve the problem when piles must have distinct stones, m≤109m≤109 and there is no constraint on sum of mm.

2140F - Sum Minimisation

Idea: shorya1835 Preparation: wakanda-forever, shorya1835

Solution

2140F - Sum Minimisation

An operation involves selecting a subset of the array of size ii (where 2≤i<n2≤i<n). This operation can cause a decrease in an element's value if and only if there exist at least two elements, ajaj and akak, in the entire array such that they are not congruent modulo ii. That is, aj≢ak(modi)aj≢ak(modi).

Let's prove this by contradiction:

  • Let's assume no operation on a subset of size ii can cause a decrease, yet there exists a pair (aj,ak)(aj,ak) such that aj≢ak(modi)aj≢ak(modi).
  • The assumption that no decrease is possible implies that for *any* subset of size ii, its sum must be divisible by ii.
  • Since i<ni<n, we can choose a subset SS of size ii that includes ajaj but excludes akak. Let its sum be ΣSΣS. By our assumption, ΣS≡0(modi)ΣS≡0(modi).
  • Now, form a new subset S′S′ by swapping ajaj with akak. The sum is ΣS′=ΣS−aj+akΣS′=ΣS−aj+ak. Again, by our assumption, this sum must also be divisible by ii, so ΣS′≡0(modi)ΣS′≡0(modi).
  • If both sums are congruent to 0 mod ii, their difference must also be.
    (ΣS−aj+ak)−ΣS≡0−0(modi)ak−aj≡0(modi)⟹aj≡ak(modi)(ΣS−aj+ak)−ΣS≡0−0(modi)ak−aj≡0(modi)⟹aj≡ak(modi)

This contradicts our initial premise that aj≢ak(modi)aj≢ak(modi) and Thus, this condition is necessary. The sufficiency is left as an exercise for the reader.

Since, you want to decrease the array sum as much as possible, you will choose operations that lead to decreasing the array indefinitely, if possible. Assume array to be sorted.

Case 11: In a2…ana2…an, if there exists a pair aj≠ak(mod2)aj≠ak(mod2), then you can perform the operations indefinitely by choosing a1a1 and ayay such that a1≠ay(mod2)a1≠ay(mod2).

Case 22: If no such pair exists, then check a2…ana2…an for all ii from 33 to n−2n−2 or if sum of a2…ana2…an is divisible by n−1n−1, if there exists some pair such that aj≠ak(modi)aj≠ak(modi) or sum is not divisible, then we can perform a series of operations to convert the array to Case 1.

Now all n−1n−1 elements are satisfied. So start considering smallest element.

Case 3.13.1: Smallest element also satisfies the condition for all mod ii (22 to n−1n−1) and sum of array is divisible by nn, then no operation leads to a decrease and answer is ∑ni=1ai∑i=1nai.

Case 3.23.2: Smallest element doesn't satisfy a mod ii condition or sum of array is not divisible by nn. If the difference in value between a1a1 and akak (modi)(modi) or ∑ni=1ai(modn)∑i=1nai(modn) is greater than 11, then we can again perform an operation to convert to case 11.

Case 3.33.3: If the difference in value between a1a1 and akak (modi)(modi) is equal to 11, then all operations including a1a1 will lead to a decrease of 11 in a1a1, after which no operation leads to a decrease and answer is ∑ni=1ai−1∑i=1nai−1.

To implement this, an easy way is to check if no operation leads to a decrease for the original array or the array after you decrease first element. To do this, use Chinese remainder theorem and check congruency of values against mod of LCM of numbers from 11 to n−1n−1 and if sum of array is divisibly by nn. This LCM is equivalent to checking if elements are equal for large nn, since ai≤109ai≤109.

Code
#include<bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
typedef vector<int> vi;
typedef vector<ll> vl;
typedef set<int> si;
typedef pair<ll, ll> pl;
#define fo(i, a, b) for (ll i = a; i < b; i++)
#define pb push_back

ll gcd(ll a,ll b){
    if(a==0)return b;
    else return gcd(b%a,a);
}
vl a;
ll lc, s, n;
bool good(){
    bool v=1;
    if(n>23){
        fo(i,0,n){
            if(a[i]!=a[0]){
                v=0;
                break;
            }
        }
    }
    else{
        fo(i,2,n){
            fo(j,0,n){
                if((a[j]%i)!=(a[0]%i)){
                    v=0;
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
    }
    v&=(s%n==0);
    return v;
}
int main() {
    cin.tie(0)->sync_with_stdio(0);
    cin.exceptions(cin.failbit);
    ll T;
    cin >> T;
    while (T--) {
        cin >> n;
        a.clear();
        a.resize(n);
        s=0;
        fo(i,0,n){
            cin >> a[i];
            s+=a[i];
        }
        sort(a.begin(),a.end());
        lc=1;
        if(n>23){
            lc=1e10;
        }
        else{
            fo(i,2,n+1){
                lc=(lc*i)/gcd(lc,i);
            }
        }
        if(good()){
            cout << s<< "\n";
            continue;
        }
        a[0]--;
        s--;
        if(good()){
            cout << s<< "\n";
            continue;
        }
        cout << -1 << "\n";


    }
}

Thanks to satyam343 for his help throughout the round.

Tutorial of Codeforces Round 1049 (Div. 2)

              OK

              instead of f(m)=∑mi=0ic⋅(m−i)n−cf(m)=∑i=0mic⋅(m−i)n−c

              it should be f(m)=∑mi=0(ic)(m−in−c)f(m)=∑i=0m(ic)(m−in−c) which to me looks like the coeficient of xm−nxm−n in 1(1−x)c+1⋅1(1−x)n−c+1=1(1−x)n+21(1−x)c+1⋅1(1−x)n−c+1=1(1−x)n+2 so it should be (n+1+m−nn+1)(n+1+m−nn+1).

    If you look at the failing 26th test case, it says:

    Yes! let we have chosen y ...d=(length of y****)+1,,and mod=x+y. Then (x#y) = x*(10^d)+y.Then x#y is divisable by x+y;it will hold iff (x*(10^d)+y)%mod=0 i.e (x*(10^d)%mod+y%mod=0 as mod>x then y%mod=y so (x*(10^d))%mod must be x to hold the criteria of (x#y)%mod=0; we already know mod>x and mod>y; so if we can make 10^d %mod==1 we can fillup the criteria....so it will become 1 when mid=10^d-1 i.e x+y=10^d-1;.....thus it is ovbious for every d<i<=9; max value of d=9,,,,we can choose choose y=999999999-x everytime and it will pass every testcase!**____**

    → Reply
  • »

    roronoa_2z
    6 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    roronoa_2z
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    We want

    (x⋅10k+y)mod(x+y)=0.(x⋅10k+y)mod(x+y)=0.

    We know

    ymod(x+y)=y.ymod(x+y)=y.

    Hence for the above to be zero, we need

    x⋅10kmod(x+y)=x.x⋅10kmod(x+y)=x.

    We also know

    xmod(x+y)=x.xmod(x+y)=x.

    Therefore

    10kmod(x+y)=1.10kmod(x+y)=1.

    A number of the form 10^k - 1 will always give remainder 1.

    As we want y to have the same number of digits as k, we take the largest possible k, which is $9$.

    → Reply

2140B - Another Divisibility Problem can be solved with other values of yy as well, for example y=999...−xy=999...−x depending on xx. Because,

x#y=x⋅10d+y=x⋅(10d−1)+(x+y)x#y=x⋅10d+y=x⋅(10d−1)+(x+y)

So, to make the 10d−110d−1, which is basically 9999...9999... for dd times, to be divisible by x+yx+y, we can make x+yx+y itself be of this shape. For xx to be any kk-digit number, x+yx+y can be of shape with any kk or more digits of 99 s. Since the constraints state that x<108x<108 and y<109y<109, we can set x+yx+y to be 999,999,999999,999,999 i.e. y=109−1−xy=109−1−x.

Also, obviously, y=8⋅xy=8⋅x works for the same reason as tutorial.

Upd: can anybody explain why the downvotes? Cause I'm pretty sure there's nothing wrong in terms of logic cause I literally got AC with this approach. 337798106

→ Reply
»
Ashwanth.K
6 months ago, show (+1) #
»
Ashwanth.K
6 months ago, hide # |
← Rev. 2 →  
+11
E2 Bonus Solution:
Spoiler

Idea:

  • Lets solve it for M=NM=N.
  • With same intuition as E2, for every x (1 <= x <= N) the count of good sequences with unique elements with final value (ans>=x)(ans>=x) can be found by (x−1)!(x−1)! (n−x+1)!(n−x+1)! cnt(n−x+1)cnt(n−x+1) where cnt(r) follows the same definition as editorial (count of masks of r bits with ans = 1)

  • From this, we could easily compute the number of permutations of n!n! with ans=xans=x (for every x from 1 to N) (i.e.) (ans≥x)−(ans≥(x+1))(ans≥x)−(ans≥(x+1))

For a general M≥1e9M≥1e9:

If we fix a integer xx as our final answer: For every r (1 to N), Say r is relative ordering of x in the array, (x is rth smallest element in the array)

  • No. of ways to choose numbers such that xx to be rr th smallest is: C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)
  • Number of good permutation sequences: ans(r)ans(r)
  • Value of its contribution: xx

We need to sum the following:

  • ∑x∗ans(r)∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)∑x∗ans(r)∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)
  • ans(r)ans(r) * ∑x∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)∑x∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)

If observed carefully, ∑x∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r)∑x∗C(x−1,r−1)∗C(m−x,n−r) when expanded on variable x, it is ∑∑ ((n+1)^th degree polynomial) , so this is a polynomial of degree n+2.

We can use interpolation, compute n+3 datapoints, and determine the value at x=mx=m in O(n2)O(n2)

So total timecomplexity O(n2n+n3)O(n2n+n3) which would pass comfortably.

Do let me know if I have made any errors, mistakes, thank you.

+3
another way to look at D

For even n, suppose that we have chosen the n/2 segments from which we take the start of the new formed segments. Call this set of segments S1 and the remaining segments S2. Then swapping any segments between these 2 sets cannot increase the answer. On solving the inequality, we get the condition that (l+r) for any segment in S2 > (l+r) for any segment in S1.

→ Reply

SlInevitable2003
6 months ago, show #
»
SlInevitable2003
6 months ago, hide # |
 
+17

My solution to Problem D works as follows: the original problem is equivalent to finding a matching among the intervals 1,⋯,n1,⋯,n that maximizes the value

∑(j,k) are matchedmax(rj−lk,rk−lj).∑(j,k) are matchedmax(rj−lk,rk−lj).

Using the identity max(A,B)=A+B2+|A−B|2max(A,B)=A+B2+|A−B|2, this expression can be rewritten as

∑(j,k)max(rj−lk,rk−lj)=∑(j,k)(rj−lj)+(rk−lk)2+∑(j,k)|(rj+lj)−(rk+lk)|2(⋆).∑(j,k)max(rj−lk,rk−lj)=∑(j,k)(rj−lj)+(rk−lk)2+∑(j,k)|(rj+lj)−(rk+lk)|2(⋆).

For even nn, the first term is constant (equal to half the total sum of the lengths of the intervals), and the second term is maximized by partitioning the array a[j]=rj+lja[j]=rj+lj into two halves and subtracting the sum of the smaller half from the sum of the larger half. This can be computed efficiently using prefix sums in O(1)O(1) time per query after preprocessing.

For odd nn, one interval (say ii) remains unmatched. We enumerate each i=1,⋯,ni=1,⋯,n as the unmatched interval, and the value in equation (⋆)(⋆) can be easily evaluated in O(1)O(1) time using a similar prefix sum approach with minor modifications, resulting to an O(n)O(n) solution.

This method is basically identical to the one in the editorial, but more clear to me.

→ Reply

can someone help me understand, why this code of mine for problem C is giving wrong answer, I am not able to understand.

//this is the code

void solve() {
    ll n;
    cin >> n;
    vector<ll> a(n);
    vector<ll> ind[2];
    map<ll, vector<ll>> mp0,mp1;
    ll cost = 0;

    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> a[i];
        ind[i % 2].push_back(a[i]);
        if (i % 2 == 0){
            cost += a[i];
            mp0[a[i]].push_back(i);
        }
        else{
            cost -= a[i];
            mp1[a[i]].push_back(i);
        }
    }

    sort(ind[0].begin(), ind[0].end()); // even paratiy
    sort(ind[1].begin(), ind[1].end()); // odd pararity

    ll temp_cost = 0;
    if (n % 2 == 0) temp_cost = n - 2;
    else temp_cost = n - 1;

    if (ind[0].empty() || ind[1].empty()) {
        cout << cost << "\n";
        return;
    }

    ll num1 = ind[1].back();   
    ll num2 = ind[0][0];       

    ll temp_cost2 = 0;
    if (!mp1[num1].empty() && !mp0[num2].empty()) {
        temp_cost2 = num1 - num2 +
            max(abs(mp1[num1][0] - mp0[num2].back()),
                abs(mp0[num2][0] - mp1[num1].back()));
    }
    ll cost2=cost;
    if (temp_cost2 >= temp_cost) {
        cost2 = cost2 + num1 - num2;
        cost2 += temp_cost2;
        //cout << cost << "\n";
    }

    cout<<max(temp_cost+cost,cost2) << "\n"; 
}
→ Reply

    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You initialize the whole DP table with 8⋅1058⋅105 elements for every test case. Since there are 104104 test cases at most, you're overwriting 8⋅1098⋅109 elements in sum, which is too slow.

    → Reply
    • »
      »
      »
      Osama-Ashraf
      6 months ago, show (+3) #
      »
      »
      »
      Osama-Ashraf
      6 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Oh, I see now. This means my array-based solution ends up doing about 2⋅1052⋅105 operations for each test case, which ignores the fact that "the sum of nn over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052⋅105". In contrast, the map-based solution only stores and processes the states that are actually needed, so its complexity is closer to O(n)O(n) overall. Is that correct?

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        _Kee
        6 months ago, show (+2) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        _Kee
        6 months ago, hide # ^ |
         
        0

        You're correct. You don't need the whole DP array for a test case with smaller nn. If you are given nn for a test case, make sure you only touch O(n)O(n) elements; then your solution won't time out.

        → Reply
        • »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Osama-Ashraf
          6 months ago, show (+1) #
          »
          »
          »
          »
          »
          Osama-Ashraf
          6 months ago, hide # ^ |
           
          0

          I see, thanks!

            »

            »

            saquib2508
            6 months ago, show #
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            »
            saquib2508
            6 months ago, hide # ^ |
             
            0

            I have faced so many WAs over the years for this reason that I always take extra care when clearing/initializing arrays. Always clear only n+5 (or 2n + 5) elements, don't touch higher indices since you don't need them.

            You'll get used to it haha. Good luck!

            → Reply
  • »
    »
    Kuroudo
    6 months ago, show (+3) #
    »
    »
    Kuroudo
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +8

    Hello Osama, i want to show you a trick you can use when you come across dp problems that involve test cases, as someone previously replied clearing your dp each case is too slow, but also the map memo isn't the best practice when it comes to this sort of problems.

    There are two main ways to to this better, first is to give each test case an id, and to distinguish if you have been in this state before in the same test case you check if vis[state] equals the current id. 338980284

    Another way is to clear exactly the size you need for this test case, after taking input n, loop and set it to the value you want.

    338981249

    Hope this helps.

    → Reply

      »
      »
      Osama-Ashraf
      6 months ago, show (+2) #
      »
      »
      »
      Osama-Ashraf
      6 months ago, hide # ^ |
       
      0

      Thanks a lot, Adham — that was really helpful. I like how these approaches let us stick with arrays instead of maps and still avoid TLE. There’s just one thing I’m not fully clear on: in the first solution, the vis array isn’t initialized. Is it safe to compare its values like that? If not, then initializing it once like int vis[(int)(2e5 + 5)][2][2]{}; should be enough, right?

This is what I got for B and it got accepted lol. If anyone could lmk why this works that would be cool. I was basing this off the fact that both these numbers will be divisible by 3 as both their digit sums would be divisible by 3 and just kinda tested from there and it worked:

#include <bits/stdc++.h>
#define PB push_back
using namespace std;
typedef long long ll;
typedef vector<int> vi;
typedef vector<long long> vll;
typedef vector<bool> vb;
typedef vector<char> vc;
typedef pair<int, int> pii;
typedef pair<long long, long long> pll;
typedef vector<pair<int, int> > vpii;
typedef vector<pair<long long, long long> > vpll;

const ll INF = 4e18;

void solve() {
  ll x;
  cin >> x; 
  string s = to_string(x);
  bool flag = false;
  if (s[0] == '9') flag = true;
  string y;
  if (flag) y.push_back('9');

  for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
    y += to_string('9'-s[i]);
  }

  cout << y << '\n';
} 

int main() {
  ios::sync_with_stdio(0);
  cin.tie(0);
  int t = 1;
  cin >> t;
  //cin.ignore(numeric_limits<streamsize>::max(), '\n');
  while(t--) {
    solve();
  }
}
→ Reply

What is wrong in this solution for D:

First we initially compute the sum of all segments , Initial_sum = S=∑ni=1yi−xiS=∑i=1nyi−xi then we want to compute the additional segments length formed by marking these segments, if we sort the lili's and riri's and then take the sum of (highest riri — least lili) + (2nd highest riri — 2nd least lili) + ..... and add this sum to the Initial_sum

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    i_squared
    6 months ago, show (+1) #
    »
    »
    i_squared
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    You probably mean the highest r_i — least l_j for distinct i and j. The problem arises in the case when a segment with the largest r_i contains all other segments. In that case we have a choice between r_i of largest segment — second highest l_i and second highest r_i — l_i of largest segment.

    Consider these segments: [1, 100], [5, 90], [98, 99]. You select the first two, but the optimal way is to take the first and the third segments.

    → Reply

    Divine_Spark
    6 months ago, show (+2) #
    »
    »
    Divine_Spark
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    For a configuration where alice wins in easy version with the bits that are 1 here are values that are >= k (for some k we are looping through) and other(0) values are < k then Alice can Force Bob into making the final Answer >=k as it forced Bob into making the final value 1 for the corresponding 0-1 mask. so we just need to count for every k the sum of masks * no of ways to generate this mask (1 filled by >=k value and 0 filled by <k values)

    → Reply

      isn't this line just the number of ways to create a mask with (n-r) places with < k and r places with >=k, cnt[r] times. So then how how are we accounting for the sum of the actual values left at last? What am i missing here?

        it is clever way of doing things, it is basically >=1 + >=2 + >= 3 ... which can be re written as 1*(==1) + 2*(==2) + 3*(==3)... which what we are required to find.

Kuroudo
6 months ago, show (+2) #
»
Kuroudo
6 months ago, hide # |
 
+7

For problem D, a very "neat" way of reaching the greedy conclusion is by expanding the formula of the chosen segments' sum. Let's name the of chosen segments CC and the rest be RR.

Our formula is :

∑i∈Cri−∑j∈Rlj∑i∈Cri−∑j∈Rlj

If we manage to eliminate contribution of group RR from this equation it will be easy to select group CC to maximize this expression. To do so we can expand the second term, based on this substitution :

∑j∈Rlj=∑ni=1li−∑k∈Clk∑j∈Rlj=∑i=1nli−∑k∈Clk

Where nn is the input size, by substitution our formula becomes :

∑i∈Cri−(∑ni=1li−∑i∈Cli)∑i∈Cri−(∑i=1nli−∑i∈Cli)
∑i∈Cri+∑i∈Cli−∑ni=1li∑i∈Cri+∑i∈Cli−∑i=1nli
∑i∈C(ri+li)−∑ni=1li∑i∈C(ri+li)−∑i=1nli

Now it's clear to see that maximizing this expression is equivalent to maximizing sum of right and left of a segment, since summation of all lefts is constant.

→ Reply

6 months ago, hide # |
 
+3

"In Problem D editorial it says you can directly find the left out segment in O(1) — can someone explain how to actually do that part?"

→ Reply
  • »
    »
    justTeri
    6 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    justTeri
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0

    When I write something/2, I mean smaller one (etc. 7/2=3), I do not know how to write that type of brackets in comment.
    Well, when n is odd, from the first sum (with all r) you need to take away one r and n/2 l+r. So, you have n choices for taking away r. Lets sort aray by l+r. Now, find a sum of all r minus sum of first n/2 l+r, I called that difference tmp. If that choice is NOT in first n/2 elements, you should just take away r. You find a minimum the same way you would normally do in an array, just with mathematical expression tmp-r.
    Now, when you search in first n/2 elements, first you take away l+r from tmp (before taking away r). But now, you substracted n/2-1 l+r's. That means you have to substract l[n/2]+r[n/2] too.
    Thats it — you can count the sum for some i in O(1) because you precalculated some "part" of the sum in tmp. You just have to be careful when examining if element is in first n/2 elements or not. For this I used two for loops because ifs looked messy.
    Hope I made it clear. :) Here is the code that worked.

    → Reply
  • »
    »
    shorya1835
    6 months ago, show #
    »
    »
    shorya1835
    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    0
    Spoiler

    After removing the first ⌊n/2⌋⌊n/2⌋ elements with the smallest li+rili+ri, we need to remove the element with the smallest rr value among the elements contributing their right end or you can keep this element and remove some element from the first ⌊n/2⌋⌊n/2⌋ elements (It's the one with the highest ll value).

    → Reply

Here's a brainf*ck solution of 2140B - Another Divisibility Problem for no reason

(Assuming all cells are 32-bit instead of 8-bit as normal, if someone can implement an 8-bit solution I'm more than excited to see it)

[-]>[-]+[[-]>[-],[+[-----------[>[-]++++++[<------>-]<--<<[->>++++++++++<<]>>[-<<+>>]<+>]]]<]<
// Inputs the number of testcases
[>
[-]>[-]+[[-]>[-],[+[-----------[>[-]++++++[<------>-]<--<<[->>++++++++++<<]>>[-<<+>>]<+>]]]<]<
// Inputs n
[->++<]>
// Calculate 2*n
>[-]>[-]+>[-]+<[>[-<-<<[->+>+<<]>[-<+>]>>]++++++++++>[-]+>[-]>[-]>[-]<<<<<
[->-[>+>>]>[[-<+>]+>+>>]<<<<<]>>-[-<<+>>]<[-]++++++++[-<++++++>]>>[-<<+>>]<<]
<[.[-]<]<
// Output 2*n
[-]++++++++++.[-]
// Output newline character
<<-
// Decrement testcase number
]
→ Reply

In the editorial for problem D, I dont understand:

this part of editorial

"The sequence of segments selected can also form a valid balanced parenthesis sequence, and thus a valid pairing."

"If the resulting sequence is not a valid balanced parenthesis sequence, then we can always improve it by swapping a segment from the left end to the right end and another from the right end to the left end."

the question

how we can swap segments without changing the assumed best possible answer in the case that the sequence is not a balanced parenthesis sequence?

      0

      I understand that part of the editorial

      as follows.

      When we finish calculating the best answer from the approach laid out in the editorial, we do not consider whether we can form a valid pairing or not, because, as stated in the editorial, that is always the case.

      And then the editorial explains why that is always the case, by first assuming we somehow have an invalid pairing at first. After which, we can fix that invalid pairing, step by step, by swapping a segment from the left-end group with a segment from the right-end group.

      When swapping 2 segments: ii from left-end group and jj from right-end group, then ansans for the new pairing should be changed by lj+ri−li−rjlj+ri−li−rj. And so, my question being: how is it possible to always find such a swapping sequence to transform an invalid pairing to a valid pairing, with the net change for ansans is 00?

      Sorry if I misunderstood the editorial. And please excuse my English as well.

      → Reply
      • »
        »
        »
        »
        shorya1835
        6 months ago, show (+1) #
        »
        »
        »
        »
        shorya1835
        6 months ago, hide # ^ |
        ← Rev. 3 →  
        0
        Spoiler

        The editorial gives the best possible upper bound for the answer, the claim is that if the bracket sequence formed for this answer is not valid, then that implies there is some right end that comes before its left end, which we can swap, but this swap leads to the answer being improved, thus, going above the upper bound, which is not possible.

        As for why a right end comes before a left end, if the sequence is not valid, that is implied from the definition of a regular bracket sequence (prefix sum has to be ≥0≥0).

        → Reply

Problem C: I'm not quite sure how you're meant to use prefix sums. Can anyone explain?

I instead (338651248) used an approach along the lines of "for each + element, what's the best — element to swap it with?". Explaining exactly how I did it is difficult, but essentially it works by considering how the distances (i.e. abs(l-r)) between + elements and — elements change as you iterate over the + elements.

for problem "another divisibility problem" doesnt x#x % x = 0? so returning x always works, nowhere in the problem can I find something that forbids you from doing that. it can be mathematically proven that x#x % x = 0:

let x be = 42 for example

4242 % 42 = (42 *10^2 + 42) % 42

42(10^2+1) % 42

this equals to zero since 10^2 +1 is an integer, that number multiplied by 42 is divisible by 42. this works for all numbers as long as 2 is substituted by the number of digits x has.

I think there's a slight logical error in Case 2 in the editorial for F. The editorial asserts that if any operation can be performed on the last n-1 elements of the array, then performing that operation will take us to Case 1. This is not true when the operation only modifies one element and that element is equal to the minimum of the array. For example, consider the array [1, 1, 3, 3]. We can perform an operation on the three larger elements, which makes the array [0, 1, 3, 3], which does not fall under Case 1.

The reason the model solution still works is that if this happens, then we can perform the same operation again, replacing the element that was decreased with the original minimum element of the array. At this point, we have two occurrences of the original minimum — 1, so we have an element other than a1a1 with the opposite parity of the elements of the original array, putting us into Case 1.

→ Reply

    shorya1835
    6 months ago, show (+3) #

    6 months ago, hide # ^ |
     
    +5

what is wrong with this approach for C-

import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
MOD = 1_000_000_007

def ans():
  n = int(input())
  l = list(map(int, input().split()))
  s = x = y = 0
  m = 1_000_000_007
  ma = -1_000_000_007

  for j in range(n):
    if j % 2 == 0:
      s += l[j]
      if (2*l[j] + j) < m:
        m, y = (2*l[j] + j), j
    else:
      s -= l[j]
      if (2*l[j] + j) > ma:
        ma, x = (2*l[j] + j), j

  xa = ya = 0
  mr = 1_000_000_007
  maa = -1_000_000_007

  for j in range(1, n+1):
    if (n - j) % 2 == 0:
      if (2*l[-j] + j - 1) < mr:
        mr, ya = (2*l[-j] + j - 1), n - j
    else:
      if (2*l[-j] + j - 1) > maa:
        maa, xa = (2*l[-j] + j - 1), n - j

  u1 = ma - m
  u2 = maa - mr  
  u = max(u1, u2)
  return s + max(u, (n - 1 - (n + 1) % 2))

for _ in range(int(input())):
  print(ans())
→ Reply
